coredata.dc:title: Boronic acid-modified fluorescent sensor using coffee biowaste-based carbon dots for the detection of dopamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Won, Sia
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine (DA) is an indispensable form of neurochemical, which normal levels are highly potential for maintaining proper brain functions. Their abnormal levels are threatening the neurological behavior in humans with aggressive Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, Huntington’s diseases, etc. Hence, in this study, a boronic acid-functionalized fluorescent sensor was developed by adapting the conveniently accessible biomass such as coffee waste for the detection of neurotransmitters like DA. In this context, we synthesized carbon dots (CDs) using coffee waste via a simple hydrothermal treatment (C-CDs). The fluorescent sensor was designed using phenylboronic acid namely 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA)-modification to the C-CDs through 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide/N-hydroxy-succinimide (EDC/NHS) coupling chemistry (B-CDs). Both C-CDs and B-CDs have shown photoluminescence (PL) emission maxima around 452 and 469 nm under the excitation maxima of 335 and 371 nm, respectively. Further, their optical properties were studied through PL response, which showed the distinct excitation-dependent and independent emission behavior, when shifting the excitation wavelengths from 300–500 nm to 300–390 nm, respectively. The C-CDs and B-CDs have displayed light and strong-blue colored emissions under ultraviolet (UV)-illumination, respectively. The B-CDs have shown fluorescence quenching against DA concentrations ranging from 0 to 30 µM with a lower detection limit of 4.25 nM. The applicability of the proposed sensor was investigated in real samples like the human serum, which exhibited good recovery values of 95.9–101.35 %.

head.ce:title: Boronic acid-modified fluorescent sensor using coffee biowaste-based carbon dots for the detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine (DA) is an indispensable form of neurochemical, which normal levels are highly potential for maintaining proper brain functions. Their abnormal levels are threatening the neurological behavior in humans with aggressive Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, Huntington’s diseases, etc. Hence, in this study, a boronic acid-functionalized fluorescent sensor was developed by adapting the conveniently accessible biomass such as coffee waste for the detection of neurotransmitters like DA. In this context, we synthesized carbon dots (CDs) using coffee waste via a simple hydrothermal treatment (C-CDs). The fluorescent sensor was designed using phenylboronic acid namely 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA)-modification to the C-CDs through 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide/N-hydroxy-succinimide (EDC/NHS) coupling chemistry (B-CDs). Both C-CDs and B-CDs have shown photoluminescence (PL) emission maxima around 452 and 469 nm under the excitation maxima of 335 and 371 nm, respectively. Further, their optical properties were studied through PL response, which showed the distinct excitation-dependent and independent emission behavior, when shifting the excitation wavelengths from 300–500 nm to 300–390 nm, respectively. The C-CDs and B-CDs have displayed light and strong-blue colored emissions under ultraviolet (UV)-illumination, respectively. The B-CDs have shown fluorescence quenching against DA concentrations ranging from 0 to 30 µM with a lower detection limit of 4.25 nM. The applicability of the proposed sensor was investigated in real samples like the human serum, which exhibited good recovery values of 95.9–101.35 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The efficient detection of neurotransmitters is highly significant in combating threatening neurological complications. Their few abnormalities may have caused multiple disorders starting from initial seizures and depression to the complex forms of disorders such as Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s disease (PD), and Schizophrenia [1]. Thus, the precise determination of neurotransmitters within a short time and greater efficiency are indispensable factors for achieving beneficial clinical diagnosis and therapeutics [2]. In this regard, dopamine (DA) is a primarily affected neurotransmitter in the substantia nigra region during Parkinsonism and again implicates therapeutic interventions [3]. So far, diverse techniques are available for its determination, which include surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), electrophoresis, chemiluminescence, chromatography, electrochemistry, and calorimetry [4–11]. However, a few shortcomings need to be addressed, which include a high budget, extended time duration, and sophisticated laborious setup.

To address these drawbacks, researchers have developed convenient fluorescence-mediated sensing methods for DA detection. These methods are improved with rapidity, superior sensitivity, certain specificity, non-toxicity, and renewability features, which are beneficial in clinical diagnosis and therapeutic options [12–14]. For instance, fluorescent sensors based on quantum dots (QDs), metal nanoclusters (NCs), and carbon dots (CDs) appropriately deliver high photostability, good dispersibility, biocompatibility, and easy synthesis features and are probed for the detection of DA [15–17]. The CDs are carbon nanomaterials with a spherical shape and most of their particle sizes are below 10 nm [18]. Additionally, CDs are represented with multifarious characteristics including generous biocompatibility, eco-friendliness, favorable thermal stability, adjustable photoluminescence (PL), and heteroatom doping [19,20]. Moreover, metal-doped CDs have shown tunable optical bandgap properties due to quantum confinement effects and thus are widely used in photocatalysis and energy storage applications [21,22]. Also, CDs are employed for several neurobiological purposes such as cell organelle-labeling, drug delivery, near-infrared (NIR) glioma-imaging, glioma-targeting, and neuronal differentiation [23–27].

Recently, numerous potentials were observed for the CDs of biomass waste origin such as abundant occurrence, low cost, non-toxicity, and biocompatibility [28]. For instance, CDs have been developed using divergent biomass wastes from a sawmill, expired milk, green pepper seeds, rice husk, tea waste, and algal blooms [29–34]. Significantly, coffee waste can be used as the major precursor for the preparation of CDs due to their desirable chemical constituents. Researchers are focusing on the enhancement of structural and chemical properties of CDs including uniform dimensionality, greater oxygenation, and morphological uniformity by producing through microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis. For example, nanocomposites were developed using poly (l-lactic acid) (PLLA)-modified coffee-CDs to induce beneficial characteristics such as high luminescence intensity, transparency, and UV-blocking capacity [35].

Recently, several functionalized CDs were developed for DA sensing using enzymatic and non-enzymatic modes through calorimetric, fluorometric, and electrochemical principles [36–41]. Unfortunately, among the reported methods, few are delivering lower sensitivity, others are requiring excess functionalization steps, and the remaining are relying on the complex maintenance of enzyme stability. In this regard, CDs consisting of specific chemical moieties i.e., boronic acid (B-OH) groups are sufficiently recognizable for 1,2-cis-diol-functional compounds like DA. However, some QDs and CDs modified by boronic acid moieties were not highly sensitive and showed their sensitivity within micromolar detection limits [15,42]. Hence, it signifies that the chemical nature and the type of functionalizing agents used to synthesize CDs are also responsible for obtaining better sensitivity results.

In this study, we developed a non-enzymatic and cost-effective fluorescent sensor from the boronic acid-functionalized coffee waste-derived CDs (i.e., B-CDs) for DA sensing. Firstly, the C-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermally-treated coffee waste, and secondly, allowed for surface-modification using 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3-APBA) via EDC/NHS reaction (i.e., B-CDs). Scheme 1 illustrates the corresponding synthesis and functionalization reactions involved in the design of final fluorescent B-CDs. Firstly-synthesized C-CDs are not sufficient for DA sensing because of inadequate recognizable functional moieties. Whereas, final B-CDs are sufficient for achieving excellent sensing potentials against DA owing to the presence of B-OH groups. These B-CDs possess several desirable characteristics, including facile synthesis, biocompatibility, non-enzymatic usage, economy, and high sensitivity. In addition, the proposed novel B-CDs exhibited excellent sensitivity in a wide linear range of nanomolar to micromolar DA concentrations. This sensing behavior is comparably superior to the formerly reported fluorescent sensors. Again, their feasibility was investigated in a real biological sample like the human serum, which suggests their future practicality in clinical settings.

Coffee waste was acquired from a local coffee shop in Seongnam City, Republic of Korea. The ethanol (70–75 %) was purchased from Dae-Jung, Republic of Korea. Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), 3-aminophenylboronic acid monohydrate (APBA), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxylsuccinimide (NHS), γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), epinephrine, norepinephrine, magnesium nitrate Mg(NO3)2, potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), glucose (Glu), lactose, galactose, mannose, fructose, uric acid (UA), glutathione (GSH), l-glutamic acid, l-cysteine (l-Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) 30 % (w/v) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The dialysis membrane (MWCO: 100–500 D) was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories, Inc. Deionized (DI) water was used for required experiments. UV–Visible spectra for B-CDs were measured using a Varian Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer at an optimal temperature of 25 °C. We have used two quartz glass cuvettes of light path of 10 mm with a volume capacity of 3.5 mL, and outside dimensions of 45 mm × 12.5 mm × 12.5 mm, inside width of 9.5 mm, and a base thickness of 1.5 mm, which are purchased from the Hellma Analytics for measuring UV–Visible spectra. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by employing a fluorescence spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, USA) consisting of a monochromator, and a Xenon lamp (Arc Lamp Housing: A-1010BTM), and a power supply (Brytexbox). The fluorescence lifetime decay measurements were performed through the Photon Technology International: EasyLife II fluorometer system and auto-adjustable excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 to 650 nm. A Thermo ScientificTM OrionTM 3-Star Benchtop pH meter was used to calibrate the pH for investigating the fluorescence stability studies of the B-CDs. The FT-IR studies were carried out through a Bruker Vertex high-resolution 70 FT-IR spectrometer. An X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-AlphaTM) consisting of a range of motion: 100–4000 eV and a 180° double-focusing hemispherical analyzer with a 128-channel detector consisting of an Al Kα micro-focused X-ray source for recording the XPS spectra of B-CDs. TEM and HRTEM images of the C-CDs and B-CDs were acquired by employing the Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200 kV electron microscope.

The C-CDs were synthesized by following the recently developed method with slight modification [43]. Firstly, the coffee waste (10 gm) was soaked in ethanol (20 mL) at room temperature about 1 h. As obtained suspension was thoroughly filtered through the Whatman Grade 1 qualitative filter paper, and collected the purified coffee suspension. This solution was concentrated around 60 °C by employing the rotary evaporator. Later, each 5 mL of the concentrated coffee extract and the 30 % (w/v) H2O2 solution were mixed in a beaker containing a 50 mL of DI water with a continuous stirring of 5 min. The resulting mixture was taken in a 100 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and treated hydrothermally for 1 h at 180 °C. Further, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature and the final solution consisting C-CDs was dialyzed by the dialysis membrane using DI water for 48 h to remove unreacted larger molecules. This solution was dried under hot-air oven at 60 °C and the resulting powder was refrigerated for further use.

The B-CDs were prepared from C-CDs using EDC/NHS coupling reaction by slight modification to the earlier method [44]. First, C-CDs (30 mg) were dispersed in a 100 mL flask containing 30 mL of pH 5.5 PBS buffer. Further, 1.5:1 M equivalents of EDC (30 mg) (15.64 mM) and NHS (12 mg) (10.42 mM) were added to the flask containing C-CDs and stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Immediately, APBA (50 mg) was added to the above mixture and continuously stirred and incubated for 24 h under the dark environment. Subsequently, the pale-yellow color of the solution was changed to brown-color and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min to remove larger particles (i.e., B-CDs). The supernatant solution containing B-CDs was dialyzed for 24 h using cellulose ester membrane (molecular weight cutoff: 3.5 kDa) and then dried under an oven. The photographs of dried powders of C-CDs and B-CDs are presented in Fig. S1 .

In a typical run, a 200 μL of B-CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) was dispersed into a cuvette consisting of PBS buffer (10 mM; pH 7.4) (400 μL) and DI water (2 mL). The resulting solution was added with individual concentrations of DA (0–30 μM) and allowed to incubate for 30 min and fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 371 nm.

The practicality of the B-CDs was investigated in the human serum to detect DA. The sample pretreatment of the human serum was carried out following the previous method with minor modifications [45]. Firstly, serum samples were centrifuged (9000 rpm; 20 min) and supernatant solutions were obtained. subsequently, a 0.5 mL of the acetonitrile was added to the above serum samples to precipitate the proteins. Again, as-obtained supernatants were centrifuged (9000 rpm; 20 min). The serum samples were diluted (50-fold) with PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The spiked samples were prepared by taking random concentrations of DA (10 and 40 nM, and 10, 20, and 30 μM), respectively. Finally, these spiked samples were utilized for the determination of DA using B-CDs.

The morphology, phase purity, and structural defects of functionalized B-CDs were investigated using TEM, XRD, and Raman spectroscopy techniques. The TEM images of B-CDs are revealing spherical structures with uniform dispersion as shown in Fig. 1
a and b. The inset HRTEM image for the B-CDs do not display any lattice fringes and confirms the amorphous morphology of the B-CDs as show in Fig. 1 b. The particle size distribution curve for the B-CDs has shown the average size of 5.8 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1 c. Interestingly, the average size of the B-CDs has increased when compared to that of C-CDs (i.e., 3.5 nm). Again, to understand the phase composition, the powder XRD analysis was investigated for the B-CDs, which demonstrate a characteristic broad peak at 20.95°, which is responsible for the (002) plane of graphitic structure ( Fig. 1 d) [46]. These results are denoting that APBA-functionalization in the C-CDs are affecting the morphology of B-CDs. The Raman spectra for the B-CDs have revealed two bands including D and G bands at 1372 and 1594 cm−1, which indicated sp3 and sp2 carbons, respectively as represented in Fig. 1 e. The high (ID/IG) intensity ratio for the B-CDs representing the presence of moderately formed graphitic CD structures [47]. These results are corroborating with the HRTEM and XRD results, which signifying the reasonably adequate graphitic nature of the functionalized B-CDs. Further, TGA studies were conducted for the both C-CDs and B-CDs in the range of 0–700 °C. The weight loss of 70.96 % and 21.35 % was observed in the C-CDs and B-CDs, respectively. The greater thermal stability was found in the B-CDs is mainly assigned from their (–NH2) and (B-OH) groups conjugated to aromatic structure. The TEM, XRD, and TGA results are shown in the Supplementary Information (
Figs. S2 and S3
).

To identify the functional groups and chemical environments of the C-CDs and B-CDs, the FT-IR analysis was conducted as shown in Fig. 2
a. The peaks at 2929 and 3267 cm−1 are attributed to (CH) and (OH) stretching vibrations, respectively. The other peaks at 1052, 1243, 1372, and 1595 cm−1 are responsible for (CN), (CC), (CO), and (CO) stretching vibrations, respectively (curve i, Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, B-CDs showed the peaks at 2900 and 2982 cm−1 are related to (CH) and the peaks at 875 and 1393 cm−1 are attributed to (CO) stretching vibrations, respectively [41]. The peaks around 1056, 1251, and 1569 cm−1 are responsible for (CN), (CC), and (CO) stretching vibrations, respectively. The B-OH stretching vibration of the APBA moiety was observed around 3208 cm−1, which corroborates with the high-resolution XPS spectra of B 1 s [48]. The additional peak at 3666 cm−1 indicates the (OH) stretching vibration (curve ii, Fig. 2 a). The broadened peak at 3267 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of C-CDs (brown square) was reduced at 3208 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs (brown circle), which suggests the APBA-functionalization of native C-CDs. A clearly visible peak was observed in the FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs at 1698 cm−1 (green circle), which corresponds to the amide (OC-N) bond [49]. These results confirmed the formation of B-CDs from C-CDs via EDC/NHS coupling reaction. For better understanding the surface functionalization process, XPS studies were investigated in the B-CDs. The total survey spectra of B-CDs are showing the different elements including O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and B 1 s as illustrated in Fig. 2 b. In the O 1 s spectra, the oxygen and carbon bonds are appeared at 531.0, 532.1, 533.5, and 534.6 eV, indicating CO, COC, C-OH, and C-OOH, respectively ( Fig. 2 c). As shown in Fig. 2 d, binding energies of 399.7 and 401.5 eV denoting the CN and OC-N bondings exist in the high-resolution spectra of N 1 s. The appearance of amide (OC-N) bond from the FT-IR and XPS spectra of the B-CDs confirms the covalent bonding between amine and carboxyl groups from APBA and C-CDs, respectively. The C 1 s spectra in Fig. 2 e have shown four deconvoluted peaks at 283.9, 285.1, 286.5, and 289.0 eV, representing the C-B, CH3, CN/CO, and CO/N-CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of B 1 s have exhibited three distinct peaks at 190.2, 191.8, and 193.5 eV, indicating B-N, BCO, and B-O bonds, respectively ( Fig. 2 f) [50]. The identified C-B, BCO, B-O, and B-N peaks are suggesting that APBA is well-bounded on the surface of B-CDs.

The optical properties of C-CDs and B-CDs were studied in DI water using UV–Visible and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. A single UV absorption peak was observed at 262 nm, which represents the π-π* transition as shown in Fig. 3
a. The PL emission property of C-CDs was investigated in DI water, which demonstrate the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 335 and 452 nm, respectively ( Fig. 3 b). The inset displays the bright-field (left side) and light blue-fluorescence (right side) photographs of the C-CDs, which are acquired in the day-light and UV-irradiation (365 nm), respectively ( Fig. 3 b). The excitation wavelength- dependent emission was delivered by the C-CDs in DI water at various excitation wavelengths including 300–500 nm ( Fig. 3 c). These results are consistent with the earlier synthesized biomass-derived CDs [51,52]. Further, optical properties of B-CDs were investigated using UV-absorption and PL studies. The low-intensity absorption peaks were observed in the UV–Visible spectrum of B-CDs at 236 and 296 nm, which are attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively ( Fig. 4
a) [42]. The PL emission property of B-CDs was studied in DI water, which exhibit the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 371 and 469 nm, respectively as shown in Fig. 4 b. There was a redshift (452 to 469 nm) in the PL emission of the B-CDs as compared to the PL emission of the C-CDs. This result is mainly due to the increase in the surface amine (NH2) functional groups in the B-CDs, which are resulted from the APBA reagent [53]. The inset shows the bright-field (left side) and strong blue-colored emission (right side) images of B-CDs under day-light and UV-illumination (365 nm), respectively. The B-CDs have shown the excitation wavelength-independent emission when dispersed in DI water under the different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 390 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 c. This property is mainly attributed to the presence of various surface states such as CO/N-CO, BCO, B-O, and B-N over the B-CDs [54]. Moreover, these results are highly consistent with the previously reported blue-colored emissive CDs [55–57].

The fluorescence response of the prepared B-CDs was studied in the ionic salt i.e., KCl with concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.0 mol/L ( Fig. S4 a). A similar PL intensity was observed in the B-CDs without fluctuations. These results are suggested that the B-CDs can be delivered with a potential colloidal stability and therefore B-CDs are significant enough to investigate in in vitro and in vivo settings [58]. To further explore the oxidative resistance property, B-CDs were investigated against fluorescence quenching behavior using H2O2 as an oxidizing agent. The B-CDs have shown the consistent fluorescence response in the H2O2 solutions (0–1.0 mM L−1) ( Fig. S4 b). Despite boronic acid moieties alone are not stable enough towards the oxidation, the formed nanostructures of B-CDs might be responsible for their enhanced acid resistance [59,60]. Later, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was studied under different pH values ranging from 3 to 12 ( Fig. S4 c). A decreased PL emission in the B-CDs was observed in extremely acidic and alkaline pH values, which were attributed to their protonation and deprotonation behaviors, respectively. Therefore, the fluorescence response of B-CDs was dependent on the pH condition. Further, the observed rise in the PL intensity of B-CDs at higher pH values may have been accredited to the abundance of ionizable functional groups on surfaces of B-CDs [61]. However, the B-CDs showed the maximum PL intensity without fluctuations at neutral pH of around 7, which is convenient for simulating the physiological pH (7.4) of the human body. In this regard, proposed B-CDs enabled a better turn-off sensing response against DA in the PBS buffer of pH 7.4. Therefore, the observed maximum fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs in the physiological pH can be beneficial to investigating biosensing applications [62]. More importantly, the resulting high sensitivity of the B-CDs against DA can be credited to the less availability of lone pair electrons on the nitrogen (N) atom in the amino group of the DA molecule at neutral pH conditions.

Finally, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was measured at different time periods starting from the synthesis to the last 120 days with an interval of 20 days (
Fig. S4
d). The corresponding inset images have shown the fluorescence stability of B-CDs without fluorescence fading, which suggested their consistency during the long-term storage under the room temperature. The fluorescence stability exhibited by the B-CDs is convenient for developing biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of DA using B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The selectivity of the B-CDs was examined against DA by conducting fluorescence experiments under the interference of metal ions (K+, Mg2+, Na+), biomolecules (UA, AA, Glu, l-Glu, GSH, and l-Cys), and neurotransmitters (GABA) as shown in the Fig. 5 c. For this study, greater concentrations of around 100 µM were utilized for interferants and around 30 µM was used for DA. Herein, a noticeable PL quenching was observed in the B-CDs against DA when compared to the above-mentioned interferants. Fortunately, the fluorescence of B-CDs was not affected by the interference of complex biomolecules, especially UA and AA. In addition, the selectivity of the prepared B-CDs was investigated over other interferants such as the cis-diol group containing saccharides (100 µM) and neurotransmitters (100 µM) (
Fig. S7
). However, there was no PL quenching was observed in the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs. Furthermore, Fig. 5 d shows the visual fluorescence images of B-CDs and concomitant interferants under daylight (topside) and UV light (365 nm) (bottom side). We believed that this high sensitivity and selectivity of B-CDs towards DA may depend on the existence of strong binding interactions between boronic acid and cis-diol groups of APBA and DA, respectively [72]. These reversible interactions can be triggered by the neutral pH of around 7.4, hence in this study, we have utilized the pH of 7.4 for detecting DA by B-CDs. These interactions may have triggered the electron transfer from B-CDs to the DA and progressed in the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs [15]. Also, this pH may have facilitated the possible PL quenching of B-CDs owing to the least plausible occurrence of lone pair electrons in the DA. Additionally, the possible assumption has been made based on the UV–vis absorption of B-CDs treated with DA as shown in Fig. 5 e. Notably, after the interaction of B-CDs with DA, a high-intensity absorption peak was observed in the UV–vis spectrum around 280 nm, which was assigned to the n-π* transitions resulting from the strong interactions between B-CDs and DA. These results are suggesting that B-CDs are suitable fluorescent nanomaterials for DA sensing. Furthermore, the detailed study of the fluorescence sensing mechanism of B-CDs against DA was investigated using the fluorescence lifetime decay curves as shown in Fig. 5 f. The double exponential model was followed for fitting these decay curves as shown in the given equation:

Where, D, τi, and ai are PL decay, lifetime, and pre-exponential factors, respectively [73,74]. The B-CDs showed the lifetimes of τ1 around 13.82 ns and τ2 around 0.035 ns without the addition of DA. However, after adding the DA, the lifetimes of B-CDs were decreased to τ1 of 11.61 ns and τ2 0.026 ns. These results are suggesting that electron transfer may have occurred in the B-CDs, which are responsible for the dynamic mode of fluorescence quenching. The possible PL quenching mechanism was illustrated for the DA sensing by the B-CDs ( Fig. 6
).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the B-CDs was studied for the determination of DA in real samples like the human serum. The spiked samples were prepared from various concentrations of DA (10 and 40 nM, and 10, 20, and 30 μM) in human serum. Table 2 summarize the recovery values of the spiked samples of DA (95.9–101.35 %). The B-CDs have shown the relative standard deviation (RSD) values in human serum ranging from 1.38 to 5.84 %. These are suggesting their feasibility in determining DA in real samples.

In this work, we synthesized blue-fluorescent CDs from coffee waste using hydrothermal reaction (C-CDs). These blue-emissive C-CDs were surface-modified with the APBA (B-CDs), which results in the exhibition of strong-blue-fluorescence for detecting DA. The developed fluorescence-based sensor was employed for DA sensing in the linear concentration range of 0–30 µM, which delivered a significant detection limit of 4.25 nM. Noteworthy, the highly selective interaction between boronic acid and catechol moieties enabled the proposed B-CDs to achieve better sensitivity towards DA detection. The quantum yields for the C-CDs and B-CDs were found to be 6.4 % and 17.5 %, respectively. The B-CDs have shown excellent stability against numerous parameters such as salts (KCl), oxidizers (H2O2), and time. The B-CDs are reasonably stable in terms of PL intensity without fluctuations at physiological pH around 7, which facilitates their potential in biological applications. Further, B-CDs were employed in human serum samples to determine DA and the obtained results are addressing the advancement of the biomass-based boronic acid-fluorescent sensor for DA sensing. We believe that the proposed sensor would contribute a valid platform for investigating other similar neurotransmitters. Altogether, B-CDs demonstrate high thermal stability and reliable sensing performance in real biological samples. Moreover, B-CDs can be practically exploited for labeling, real-time tracking, and thorough visual monitoring of significant neurotransmitters within the brain. Also, in the future, we will study the developed sensor for the in vitro assessment of DA using relevant neuro-related cell lines.

Roopkumar Sangubotla: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Sia Won: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jongsung Kim: Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.
